A security controls framework by the Cloud Security Alliance (CSA) that provides a detailed set of cloud-specific security controls mapped to major compliance standards
A network routing table for cloud traffic
A matrix of cloud service models and deployment types
questions.explanation: The CSA Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM) is a cybersecurity controls framework designed for cloud computing. It maps cloud-specific security controls to major standards (ISO 27001, NIST, PCI DSS), helping organizations assess and manage cloud security.

questions.question: What is a right to audit clause in a cloud service agreement?
options: The CSP's right to audit the customer
A contractual provision that grants the customer the right to audit or have a third party audit the CSP's security controls and compliance
The right to review cloud billing statements
The right to audit other customers' data
questions.explanation: A right to audit clause gives the customer (or their designated third party) the contractual right to examine the CSP's security controls, processes, and compliance. For large CSPs, this is often satisfied through SOC 2 or ISO 27001 reports.

questions.question: What is the CSA STAR (Security, Trust, Assurance, and Risk) program?
options: A cloud pricing comparison tool
A program that provides a publicly accessible registry where cloud providers document their security controls, enabling transparency and assurance
A cloud deployment methodology
A vulnerability scanning tool
questions.explanation: CSA STAR is a tiered program where cloud providers publish self-assessments (Level 1), third-party audits (Level 2), or continuous monitoring results (Level 3) of their security controls using the CCM framework, promoting transparency.

questions.question: What is the significance of data residency requirements in cloud computing?
options: Data must be stored in the most affordable region
Certain regulations require that data physically resides within specific geographic boundaries, affecting cloud region selection and replication strategies
Data residency only applies to on-premises storage
Data can be stored anywhere without restrictions
questions.explanation: Data residency requirements mandate that certain data (often personal data) must be stored within specific countries or regions. This affects cloud architecture decisions including region selection, replication, backup locations, and CDN configurations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a service level agreement (SLA) with a cloud provider?
options: To define the cloud architecture
To formally define the expected level of service including uptime guarantees, performance metrics, and remedies for non-compliance
To set employee work schedules
To design the application user interface
questions.explanation: An SLA formally defines service expectations between the customer and CSP, including uptime guarantees (e.g., 99.99%), performance metrics, support response times, and financial remedies (credits) when commitments are not met.

questions.question: What is the difference between SOC 2 Type I and SOC 2 Type II reports?
options: There is no difference
Type I evaluates the design of controls at a point in time; Type II evaluates the design AND operating effectiveness of controls over a period of time (typically 6-12 months)
Type I is for cloud providers; Type II is for on-premises only
Type II is less rigorous than Type I
questions.explanation: SOC 2 Type I assesses whether controls are properly designed at a specific point in time. SOC 2 Type II evaluates both design and operating effectiveness over a period (6-12 months), providing stronger assurance that controls actually work consistently.

questions.question: What is PCI DSS, and when does it apply to cloud environments?
options: A cloud networking protocol
The Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard, which applies when cloud environments store, process, or transmit cardholder data
A data encryption standard used only in private clouds
A cloud provider certification program
questions.explanation: PCI DSS applies to any entity that stores, processes, or transmits payment card data. When cardholder data is in the cloud, both the customer and the relevant CSP components must comply with PCI DSS requirements.
